subject: ap-world-history
title: South & Southeast Asia
stimulus:
"[A]lready well before the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, an enormous amount of [history] had been written, and Muslims were well on their way to generating a tradition that outstripped in size and quality anything written by . . . Jews or Christians. The impulse [for so much history writing] was two-fold. [First], the spectacular success of empire building during the seventh and eighth centuries created a market of readers hungry for historical narrative, . . . which offered lessons and models to rulers, their courts, and urban elites. . . . Second, . . . many were enthusiastic about those forms [of history writing] that reinforced traditional religious institutions . . . and attitudes. In practice, this meant that they wrote a great many [biographies] of religious figures and of the Prophet."

Source: Tarif Khalidi, scholar of Islamic Studies at Harvard University, "Islamic Historiography: The Histories of Mas'udi," 1975

parts.prompt: Identify ONE argument that the author makes in the first paragraph.
parts.scoring_notes: [Note: This may be credited for either part (a) or part (b) but not for both.]

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying one argument the author makes in the first paragraph. Note: the argument that Muslims wrote more history than Jews and Christians may be credited for either part (a) or part (b) but not both. Acceptable responses include: Muslims wrote more history than Jews and Christians in the 12th and 13th centuries; the establishment of Islamic caliphates in the 7th and 8th centuries was a factor in the enormous amount of history writing; Muslim states and elites promoted history writing; Muslim readers wanted historical narratives about religious and political figures.

sample_responses.response: The author argues that the establishment of Islamic caliphates in the seventh and eighth centuries created a market of readers hungry for historical narratives about rulers, courts, and urban elites, which drove the enormous volume of Islamic history writing.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific argument from the first paragraph — the two-fold impulse for history writing — connecting empire-building to reader demand for historical narrative.

sample_responses.response: The author claims that Muslim states and elites actively promoted history writing because it offered lessons and models for rulers.
sample_responses.commentary: Another acceptable argument from the first paragraph — rulers patronized history as a form of political education and legitimation.

sample_responses.response: The passage is about Islamic history writing.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the topic rather than identifying a specific argument. Does not earn the point.

required_elements: Muslims wrote more history than Jews and Christians in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries.
alternatives.correct_example: Muslims wrote more history than Jews and Christians in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt: Identify ONE piece of evidence that the author uses to support an argument in the passage.
parts.scoring_notes: [Note: This may be credited for either part (a) or part (b) but not for both.]

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying one piece of evidence the author uses to support an argument in the passage. Note: the comparative point about Muslim vs. Jewish/Christian history writing may be credited for either part (a) or part (b) but not both. Acceptable responses: the author states that Muslim historians wrote biographies of the Prophet Muhammad and other religious figures to reinforce traditional religious institutions; rulers, their courts, and urban elites were the main audiences for Muslim histories.

sample_responses.response: The author uses the example of Muslim historians writing biographies of the Prophet Muhammad and other religious figures as evidence that history writing served to reinforce traditional religious institutions.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies a specific piece of evidence from the passage — the biography tradition (sira literature) — and connects it to the author's argument about the religious impulse for history writing.

sample_responses.response: The author argues that rulers, their courts, and urban elites were the main audiences for Muslim histories, which explains why so much Islamic history writing was produced.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the audience data as a piece of evidence supporting the argument about elite patronage of history writing.

sample_responses.response: Muslims wrote a lot of history, which proves the author's argument.
sample_responses.commentary: States the general claim of the passage rather than identifying a specific piece of evidence the author uses to support that claim.

required_elements: The author states that many Muslim historians wrote biographies of the Prophet Muhammad and other religious figures to reinforce traditional religious institutions.
alternatives.correct_example: The author states that many Muslim historians wrote biographies of the Prophet Muhammad and other religious figures to reinforce traditional religious institutions.

required_elements: The author says that during the twelfth and thirteenth centuries Muslims wrote more histories than Christians or Jews wrote.
alternatives.correct_example: The author says that during the twelfth and thirteenth centuries Muslims wrote more histories than Christians or Jews wrote.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain how ONE development in the period 1200–1750 could be used to support the author’s
argument in the second paragraph about history writing and art creation legitimizing states’
exercise of power.
2
Graduation Ceremony at a Christian Theological Seminary in Northern India, 1897
© The Trustees of the British Museum
The image shows two British missionaries (second row, center) surrounded by Indian graduates. The
seminary (religious school) was founded in 1871 to educate and train Indian missionaries.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining how one development in 1200–1750 supports the author's argument that history writing and art creation legitimized states' exercise of power. Must go beyond the passage with a causal explanation. Acceptable responses: rulers sponsored historical writing praising their dynasties to increase political legitimacy; the Abbasid Caliphate supported scholars in the House of Wisdom to legitimize their power; rulers used religious ideas, art, and monumental architecture to legitimize rule.

sample_responses.response: The Abbasid Caliphate sponsored scholars at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad to translate Greek texts and produce original historical and scientific works, which helped legitimize Abbasid rule by demonstrating their role as patrons of Islamic learning and custodians of a sophisticated civilization — supporting the author's argument that Muslim states actively promoted history writing to exercise and justify power.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific historical development (Abbasid House of Wisdom) and explains how it supports the author's argument about history writing as a tool of political legitimation. CB-aligned example.

sample_responses.response: Ottoman sultans commissioned official court chronicles that praised the dynasty's military conquests and divine favor, as well as monumental architecture like the Hagia Sophia conversion and the Süleymaniye Mosque, to demonstrate their legitimacy as Islamic rulers — showing how art and writing served state power as the author argues.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses a specific development (Ottoman court chronicles and monumental architecture) to support the author's argument about state-sponsored cultural production legitimizing rule.

sample_responses.response: Rulers in the period 1200–1750 used art to show they were powerful.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not identify a specific development and does not explain the causal connection to legitimizing power.

required_elements: Rulers often sponsored historical writing that praised the actions of their dynasties and ancestors to increase the political legitimacy of their state.
alternatives.correct_example: Rulers often sponsored historical writing that praised the actions of their dynasties and ancestors to increase the political legitimacy of their state.

required_elements: The Abbasid Caliphate supported scholars in the House of Wisdom in Baghdad to legitimize their power.
alternatives.correct_example: The Abbasid Caliphate supported scholars in the House of Wisdom in Baghdad to legitimize their power.

required_elements: Rulers used religious ideas, art, and monumental architecture to legitimize their rule.
alternatives.correct_example: Rulers used religious ideas, art, and monumental architecture to legitimize their rule.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.
